## Why `ReadWriteHTMLTestMixin` Exists and Why Defaults Compensate for Missing Columns

### The Mixin Class

**`ReadWriteHTMLTestMixin`** is defined in `astropy/cosmology/_src/tests/io/test_html.py` (lines 19–227). It inherits from `ReadWriteTestMixinBase` (`io/base.py:53`) and is designed to be mixed into concrete test classes — its name doesn't start with `Test`, so pytest ignores it unless inherited.

Its purpose is to verify the full round-trip of `Cosmology` instances through `format="ascii.html"` (HTML tables via BeautifulSoup4), covering write → read → equivalence, LaTeX column name conversion, multi-row indexing, and crucially — **reading back tables with missing columns**.

---

### The Core Scenario: Missing Columns

The key test is `test_readwrite_html_subclass_partial_info()` (`test_html.py:144–177`):

```python
def test_readwrite_html_subclass_partial_info(self, cosmo_cls, cosmo, read, write, ...):
    write(fp, format="ascii.html", latex_names=latex_names)

    tbl = QTable.read(fp)
    cname = "$$T_{0}$$" if latex_names else "Tcmb0"
    del tbl[cname]          # simulate a missing column
    tbl.write(fp, overwrite=True)

    got = cosmo_cls.read(fp, format="ascii.html")
    # ...
    assert got.Tcmb0 == cosmo_cls.parameters["Tcmb0"].default
```

It manually deletes the `Tcmb0` column from the table before reading, then asserts that the read-back object fills it with the **class-level default** rather than failing.

---

### Why Defaults Must Compensate

The mechanism that makes this work is in `astropy/cosmology/_src/io/builtin/mapping.py` (the `from_mapping()` function, lines ~277–282):

```python
sig = inspect.signature(cosmology)
ba = sig.bind_partial()    # start with empty args
ba.apply_defaults()        # fill ALL defaults first
for k in sig.parameters.keys():
    if k in params:        # overwrite with table data where present
        ba.arguments[k] = params.pop(k)
```

`bind_partial()` + `apply_defaults()` pre-populates every parameter with its class default. Then only parameters actually present in the HTML table overwrite those defaults. Any column absent from the HTML silently falls back to the class default.

---

### Why the Mixin Exists to Test This

HTML is a human-editable format. Unlike binary formats, users routinely open HTML tables and:
- Delete columns they don't care about
- Share partial tables across different cosmology class versions

Without the default-filling behavior, every such edit would cause a hard failure on read. The mixin exists to **guarantee this contract doesn't regress**: if you read an HTML table with class `FlatLambdaCDM` and `Tcmb0` is absent, you always get `Tcmb0 = FlatLambdaCDM.parameters["Tcmb0"].default` (0 K), not an exception.

The test also validates internal consistency (`got == got2 == got3`) across three equivalent read paths and asserts the reconstructed cosmology differs from the original only in the deleted parameter — proving the default substitution is precise and isolated.

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_0195Ny6i353JCrqQ9Q2FVjWF","type":"tool_result","content":"1	# Licensed under a 3-clause BSD style license - see LICENSE.rst
2	"""|Cosmology| <-> |Table| I/O, using |Cosmology.to_format| and |Cosmology.from_format|.
3	
4	This module provides functions to transform a |Cosmology| object to and from a |Table|
5	object. The functions are registered with ``convert_registry`` under the format name
6	"astropy.table". |Table| itself has an abundance of I/O methods, making this conversion
7	useful for further interoperability with other formats.
8	
9	A Cosmology as a `~astropy.table.QTable` will have the cosmology's name and parameters
10	as columns.
11	
12	    >>> from astropy.cosmology import Planck18
13	    >>> ct = Planck18.to_format("astropy.table")
14	    >>> ct
15	    <QTable length=1>
16	        name        H0        Om0    Tcmb0    Neff      m_nu      Ob0
17	                km / (Mpc s)            K                 eV
18	        str8     float64    float64 float64 float64  float64[3] float64
19	    -------- ------------ ------- ------- ------- ----------- -------
20	    Planck18        67.66 0.30966  2.7255   3.046 0.0 .. 0.06 0.04897
21	
22	The cosmological class and other metadata, e.g. a paper reference, are in the Table's
23	metadata.
24	
25	    >>> ct.meta
26	    {..., 'cosmology': 'FlatLambdaCDM'}
27	
28	
29	Cosmology supports the astropy Table-like protocol (see :ref:`Table-like Objects`) to
30	the same effect:
31	
32	.. code-block::
33	
34	    >>> from astropy.table import QTable
35	    >>> QTable(Planck18)
36	    <QTable length=1>
37	      name        H0        Om0    Tcmb0    Neff      m_nu      Ob0
38	             km / (Mpc s)            K                 eV
39	      str8     float64    float64 float64 float64  float64[3] float64
40	    -------- ------------ ------- ------- ------- ----------- -------
41	    Planck18        67.66 0.30966  2.7255   3.046 0.0 .. 0.06 0.04897
42	
43	
44	To move the cosmology class from the metadata to a Table row, set the
45	``cosmology_in_meta`` argument to `False`:
46	
47	    >>> Planck18.to_format("astropy.table", cosmology_in_meta=False)
48	    <QTable length=1>
49	        cosmology     name        H0        Om0    Tcmb0    Neff      m_nu      Ob0
50	                            km / (Mpc s)            K                 eV
51	        str13       str8     float64    float64 float64 float64  float64[3] float64
52	    ------------- -------- ------------ ------- ------- ------- ----------- -------
53	    FlatLambdaCDM Planck18        67.66 0.30966  2.7255   3.046 0.0 .. 0.06 0.04897
54	
55	Astropy recommends `~astropy.table.QTable` for tables with |Quantity| columns. However
56	the returned type may be overridden using the ``cls`` argument:
57	
58	    >>> from astropy.table import Table
59	    >>> Planck18.to_format("astropy.table", cls=Table)
60	    <Table length=1>
61	    ...
62	
63	Fields of the cosmology may be renamed using the ``rename`` argument.
64	
65	    >>> Planck18.to_format("astropy.table", rename={"H0": "Hubble"})
66	    <QTable length=1>
67	        name      Hubble      Om0    Tcmb0    Neff      m_nu      Ob0
68	                km / (Mpc s)            K                 eV
69	        str8     float64    float64 float64 float64  float64[3] float64
70	    -------- ------------ ------- ------- ------- ----------- -------
71	    Planck18        67.66 0.30966  2.7255   3.046 0.0 .. 0.06 0.04897
72	
73	Appropriately formatted tables can be converted to |Cosmology| instances. Since the
74	|Table| can hold arbitrary metadata, we can faithfully round-trip a |Cosmology| through
75	|Table|, e.g. to construct a ``Planck18`` cosmology identical to the instance from which
76	it was generated.
77	
78	    >>> ct = Planck18.to_format("astropy.table")
79	
80	    >>> cosmo = Cosmology.from_format(ct, format="astropy.table")
81	    >>> cosmo
82	    FlatLambdaCDM(name='Planck18', H0=<Quantity 67.66 km / (Mpc s)>, Om0=0.30966, Tcmb0=<Quantity 2.7255 K>, Neff=3.046, m_nu=<Quantity [0.  , 0.  , 0.06] eV>, Ob0=0.04897)
83	    >>> cosmo == Planck18
84	    True
85	
86	The ``cosmology`` information (row or metadata) may be omitted if the cosmology class
87	(or its string name) is passed as the ``cosmology`` keyword argument to
88	|Cosmology.from_format|.
89	
90	    >>> del ct.meta["cosmology"]  # remove cosmology from metadata
91	    >>> Cosmology.from_format(ct, cosmology="FlatLambdaCDM")
92	    FlatLambdaCDM(name='Planck18', H0=<Quantity 67.66 km / (Mpc s)>, Om0=0.30966, Tcmb0=<Quantity 2.7255 K>, Neff=3.046, m_nu=<Quantity [0.  , 0.  , 0.06] eV>, Ob0=0.04897)
93	
94	Alternatively, specific cosmology classes can be used to parse the data.
95	
96	    >>> from astropy.cosmology import FlatLambdaCDM
97	    >>> FlatLambdaCDM.from_format(ct)
98	     FlatLambdaCDM(name='Planck18', H0=<Quantity 67.66 km / (Mpc s)>, Om0=0.30966, Tcmb0=<Quantity 2.7255 K>, Neff=3.046, m_nu=<Quantity [0.  , 0.  , 0.06] eV>, Ob0=0.04897)
99	
100	When using a specific cosmology class, the class' default parameter values are used to
101	fill in any missing information.
102	
103	    >>> del ct["Tcmb0"]  # show FlatLambdaCDM provides default
104	    >>> FlatLambdaCDM.from_format(ct)
105	     FlatLambdaCDM(name='Planck18', H0=<Quantity 67.66 km / (Mpc s)>, Om0=0.30966, Tcmb0=<Quantity 0. K>, Neff=3.046, m_nu=None, Ob0=0.04897)
106	
107	For tables with multiple rows of cosmological parameters, the ``index`` argument is
108	needed to select the correct row. The index can be an integer for the row number or, if
109	the table is indexed by a column, the value of that column. If the table is not indexed
110	and ``index`` is a string, the "name" column is used as the indexing column.
111	
112	Here is an example where ``index`` is needed and can be either an integer (for the row
113	number) or the name of one of the cosmologies, e.g. 'Planck15'.
114	
115	    >>> from astropy.cosmology import Planck13, Planck15, Planck18
116	    >>> from astropy.table import vstack
117	    >>> cts = vstack([c.to_format("astropy.table")
118	    ...               for c in (Planck13, Planck15, Planck18)],
119	    ...              metadata_conflicts='silent')
120	    >>> cts
121	    <QTable length=3>
122	        name        H0        Om0    Tcmb0    Neff      m_nu      Ob0
123	                km / (Mpc s)            K                 eV
124	        str8     float64    float64 float64 float64  float64[3]  float64
125	    -------- ------------ ------- ------- ------- ----------- --------
126	    Planck13        67.77 0.30712  2.7255   3.046 0.0 .. 0.06 0.048252
127	    Planck15        67.74  0.3075  2.7255   3.046 0.0 .. 0.06   0.0486
128	    Planck18        67.66 0.30966  2.7255   3.046 0.0 .. 0.06  0.04897
129	
130	    >>> cosmo = Cosmology.from_format(cts, index=1, format="astropy.table")
131	    >>> cosmo == Planck15
132	    True
133	
134	Fields in the table can be renamed to match the `~astropy.cosmology.Cosmology` class'
135	signature using the ``rename`` argument. This is useful when the table's column names do
136	not match the class' parameter names.
137	
138	    >>> renamed_table = Planck18.to_format("astropy.table", rename={"H0": "Hubble"})
139	    >>> renamed_table
140	    <QTable length=1>
141	        name      Hubble      Om0    Tcmb0    Neff      m_nu      Ob0
142	                km / (Mpc s)            K                 eV
143	        str8     float64    float64 float64 float64  float64[3] float64
144	    -------- ------------ ------- ------- ------- ----------- -------
145	    Planck18        67.66 0.30966  2.7255   3.046 0.0 .. 0.06 0.04897
146	
147	    >>> cosmo = Cosmology.from_format(renamed_table, format="astropy.table",
148	    ...                               rename={"Hubble": "H0"})
149	    >>> cosmo == Planck18
150	    True
151	"""
152

302	    if isinstance(index, str):

307	                    if value == "name":

311	            indices = np.where(table[nc] == index)[0]
312	        else:  # has indexing column
313	            indices = table.loc_indices[index]  # need to convert to row index (int)

315	        if isinstance(indices, (int, np.integer)):  # loc_indices
316	            index = indices

332	            index = 0

377	    --------
378	    A Cosmology as a `~astropy.table.QTable` will have the cosmology's name and
379	    parameters as columns.

381	        >>> from astropy.cosmology import Planck18

386	                 km / (Mpc s)            K                 eV

388	        -------- ------------ ------- ------- ------- ----------- -------
389	        Planck18        67.66 0.30966  2.7255   3.046 0.0 .. 0.06 0.04897

403	                               km / (Mpc s)            K                 eV
404	            str13       str8     float64    float64 float64 float64  float64[3] float64
405	        ------------- -------- ------------ ------- ------- ------- ----------- -------
406	        FlatLambdaCDM Planck18        67.66 0.30966  2.7255   3.046 0.0 .. 0.06 0.04897
407	
408	    Astropy recommends `~astropy.table.QTable` for tables with |Quantity| columns.

417

419	        <QTable length=1>

421	                 km / (Mpc s)            K                 eV
422	          str8     float64    float64 float64 float64  float64[3] float64
423	        -------- ------------ ------- ------- ------- ----------- -------
424	        Planck18        67.66 0.30966  2.7255   3.046 0.0 .. 0.06 0.04897
425	    """
426	    if not issubclass(cls, Table):
427	        raise TypeError(f"'cls' must be a (sub)class of Table, not {type(cls)}")

429	    # Start by getting a map representation.
430	    data = to_mapping(cosmology)

432	
433	    # Metadata

435	    if cosmology_in_meta:

439	    # - Column for Parameter

3	
4	This module provides functions to transform a |Cosmology| instance to a mapping
5	(`dict`-like) object and vice versa, from a mapping object back to a |Cosmology|
6	instance. The functions are registered with ``convert_registry`` under the format name
7	"mapping". The mapping object is a `dict`-like object, with the cosmology's parameters
8	and metadata as items. `dict` is a fundamental data structure in Python, and this
9	representation of a |Cosmology| is useful for translating between many serialization and
10	storage formats, or even passing arguments to functions.
11	
12	We start with the simple case of outputting a |Cosmology| as a mapping.
13	
14	    >>> from astropy.cosmology import Cosmology, Planck18
15	    >>> cm = Planck18.to_format('mapping')
16	    >>> cm
17	    {'cosmology': <class 'astropy.cosmology...FlatLambdaCDM'>,
18	     'name': 'Planck18', 'H0': <Quantity 67.66 km / (Mpc s)>, 'Om0': 0.30966,
19	     'Tcmb0': <Quantity 2.7255 K>, 'Neff': 3.046,
20	     'm_nu': <Quantity [0. , 0. , 0.06] eV>, 'Ob0': 0.04897,
21	     'meta': ...
22	
23	``cm`` is a `dict`, with the cosmology's parameters and metadata as items.
24	
25	How might we use this `dict`? One use is to unpack the `dict` into a function:
26	
27	    >>> def function(H0, Tcmb0, **kwargs): ...
28	    >>> function(**cm)
29	
30	Another use is to merge the `dict` with another `dict`:
31	
32	    >>> cm2 = {'H0': 70, 'Tcmb0': 2.7}
33	    >>> cm | cm2
34	    {'cosmology': <class 'astropy.cosmology...FlatLambdaCDM'>, ..., 'H0': 70, ...}
35	
36	Most saliently, the `dict` can also be used to construct a new cosmological instance
37	identical to the |Planck18| cosmology from which it was generated.
38	
39	    >>> cosmo = Cosmology.from_format(cm, format="mapping")
40	    >>> cosmo
41	    FlatLambdaCDM(name='Planck18', H0=<Quantity 67.66 km / (Mpc s)>, Om0=0.30966, Tcmb0=<Quantity 2.7255 K>, Neff=3.046, m_nu=<Quantity [0.  , 0.  , 0.06] eV>, Ob0=0.04897)
42	
43	How did |Cosmology.from_format| know to return an instance of the |FlatLambdaCDM| class?
44	The mapping object has a field ``cosmology`` which can be either the string name of the
45	cosmology class (e.g. "FlatLambdaCDM") or the class itself.
46	
47	This field can be omitted under two conditions.
48	
49	1. If the cosmology class is passed as the ``cosmology`` keyword argument to
50	   |Cosmology.from_format|,
51	2. If a specific cosmology class, e.g. |FlatLambdaCDM|, is used to parse the data.
52	
53	To the first point, we can pass the cosmology class as the ``cosmology`` keyword
54	argument to |Cosmology.from_format|.
55	
56	    >>> del cm["cosmology"]  # remove cosmology class
57	
58	    >>> Cosmology.from_format(cm, cosmology="FlatLambdaCDM")
59	    FlatLambdaCDM(name='Planck18', H0=<Quantity 67.66 km / (Mpc s)>, Om0=0.30966, Tcmb0=<Quantity 2.7255 K>, Neff=3.046, m_nu=<Quantity [0.  , 0.  , 0.06] eV>, Ob0=0.04897)
60	
61	To the second point, we can use specific cosmology class to parse the data.
62	
63	    >>> from astropy.cosmology import FlatLambdaCDM
64	    >>> FlatLambdaCDM.from_format(cm)
65	    FlatLambdaCDM(name='Planck18', H0=<Quantity 67.66 km / (Mpc s)>, Om0=0.30966, Tcmb0=<Quantity 2.7255 K>, Neff=3.046, m_nu=<Quantity [0.  , 0.  , 0.06] eV>, Ob0=0.04897)
66	
67	Also, the class' default parameter values are used to fill in any information missing in
68	the data. For example, if ``Tcmb0`` is missing, the default value of 0.0 K is used.
69	
70	    >>> del cm["Tcmb0"]  # show FlatLambdaCDM provides default
71	    >>> FlatLambdaCDM.from_format(cm)
72	    FlatLambdaCDM(name='Planck18', H0=..., Tcmb0=<Quantity 0. K>, ...)
73	
74	If instead of *missing* information, there is *extra* information, there are a few
75	options. The first is to use the ``move_to_meta`` keyword argument to move fields that
76	are not in the Cosmology constructor to the Cosmology's metadata.
77	
78	    >>> cm2 = cm | {"extra": 42, "cosmology": "FlatLambdaCDM"}
79	    >>> cosmo = Cosmology.from_format(cm2, move_to_meta=True)
80	    >>> cosmo.meta
81	    {'extra': 42, ...}
82	
83	Alternatively, the ``rename`` keyword argument can be used to rename keys in the mapping
84	to fields of the |Cosmology|. This is crucial when the mapping has keys that are not
85	valid arguments to the |Cosmology| constructor.
86	
87	    >>> cm3 = dict(cm)  # copy
88	    >>> cm3["cosmo_cls"] = "FlatLambdaCDM"
89	    >>> cm3["cosmo_name"] = cm3.pop("name")
90	
91	    >>> rename = {'cosmo_cls': 'cosmology', 'cosmo_name': 'name'}
92	    >>> Cosmology.from_format(cm3, rename=rename)
93	    FlatLambdaCDM(name='Planck18', H0=<Quantity 67.66 km / (Mpc s)>, Om0=0.30966, Tcmb0=<Quantity 0. K>, Neff=3.046, m_nu=None, Ob0=0.04897)
94	
95	Let's take a closer look at |Cosmology.to_format|, because there a lot of options, to
96	tailor the output to specific needs.
97	
98	The dictionary type may be changed with the ``cls`` keyword argument:
99	
100	    >>> from collections import OrderedDict
101	    >>> Planck18.to_format('mapping', cls=OrderedDict)
102	    OrderedDict({'cosmology': <class 'astropy.cosmology...FlatLambdaCDM'>, 'name': 'Planck18', 'H0': <Quantity 67.66 km / (Mpc s)>, 'Om0': 0.30966, 'Tcmb0': <Quantity 2.7255 K>, 'Neff': 3.046, 'm_nu': <Quantity [0.  , 0.  , 0.06] eV>, 'Ob0': 0.04897, 'meta': {...}})
103

105	itself. The keyword argument ``cosmology_as_str`` may be used:
106	
107	    >>> Planck18.to_format('mapping', cosmology_as_str=True)

117	    >>> Planck18.to_format('mapping', move_from_meta=True)
118	    {'cosmology': <class 'astropy.cosmology...FlatLambdaCDM'>,
119	        'name': 'Planck18', 'Oc0': 0.2607, 'n': 0.9665, 'sigma8': 0.8102, ...

122	
123	    >>> rename = {'cosmology': 'cosmo_cls', 'name': 'cosmo_name'}
124	    >>> Planck18.to_format('mapping', rename=rename)

143	def _rename_map(
144	    map: Mapping[str, Any], /, renames: Mapping[str, str]
145	) -> dict[str, Any]:
146	    """Apply rename to map."""
147	    if common_names := set(renames.values()).intersection(map):
148	        raise ValueError(
149	            "'renames' values must be disjoint from 'map' keys, "
150	            f"the common keys are: {common_names}"
151	        )
152	    return {renames.get(k, k): v for k, v in map.items()}  # dict separate from input

184	        class (e.g. "FlatLambdaCDM") or the class itself.

204	    `~astropy.cosmology.Cosmology` subclass instance

206	    Examples
207	    --------

210	    :meth:`~astropy.cosmology.Cosmology.to_format`.

217	         'Tcmb0': <Quantity 2.7255 K>, 'Neff': 3.046,

219	         'meta': ...

222	    |Planck18| cosmology from which it was generated.
223	
224	        >>> cosmo = Cosmology.from_format(cm, format="mapping")
225	        >>> cosmo
226	        FlatLambdaCDM(name='Planck18', H0=<Quantity 67.66 km / (Mpc s)>, Om0=0.30966, Tcmb0=<Quantity 2.7255 K>, Neff=3.046, m_nu=<Quantity [0.  , 0.  , 0.06] eV>, Ob0=0.04897)
227	
228	    The ``cosmology`` field can be omitted if the cosmology class (or its string name)
229	    is passed as the ``cosmology`` keyword argument to |Cosmology.from_format|.
230	
231	        >>> del cm["cosmology"]  # remove cosmology class
232	        >>> Cosmology.from_format(cm, cosmology="FlatLambdaCDM")

236	
237	        >>> from astropy.cosmology import FlatLambdaCDM
238	        >>> FlatLambdaCDM.from_format(cm)
